content:
What do we put in our safety comments for cases where a call requires a certain context, and we expect klint to catch that in the future?
e.g.: https://lore.kernel.org/all/87wmpxghxg.ffs@tglx/

I think that you would have to annotate that function with a requirement by klint, then you could write "due to the attribute on this function, klint guarantees that it can only be called from non-atomic contexts". But that is only possible when we actually have the klint attributes.
Indeed. So what should I do today?
the safe way would be to make the function unsafe and ask for the correct context in the requirements.
I don't know how often you need to call ktime_get, if it is not that often, I would do that.
I don't think it is realistic to make all of our sleeping functions unsafe.
Even if it's temporary.
ah you are right, we do have other functions where this is also an issue

then I think adding a "FIXME(klint): add klint attribute once available" at the top of the function and maybe something along the lines of "SAFETY: in the future, klint will guarantee that this is only callable from non-atomic contexts. At the moment this is ensured through code review."?
Okay.
what do the others think?
Though in lots of other cases, we have just ignored these problems entirely.
yeah, but that was also historical, since we did not know about the issue IIRC
There are definitely cases that have been added after we found out about it :)
yes, we should go through and add some fixmes there too.
I can try to take a look, but currently busy with pinned_init
I agree, execution context related safety requirements should be checked by klint.
since, otherwise it's going to be very challenging to have a safe version of the function.

btw, could you introduce a minimal version of Instant<KernelTime> (backed by a ktime_t) whose elapsed function returns a Ktime? In other words, Ktime is the kernel version of Duration and we differentiate a time stamp vs a time duration.

Benno Lossin said:

I think that you would have to annotate that function with a requirement by klint, then you could write "due to the attribute on this function, klint guarantees that it can only be called from non-atomic contexts". But that is only possible when we actually have the klint attributes.

Is this currently blocked on something in the compiler or is it just a matter of someone finding some spare cycles to do it?
I don't know, maybe @Gary Guo can tell you more?
Klint needs to do more accurate analysis before we start using it
